topic: dry season worries — water levels low

turns.content: the stream is low my friend. very low. I have not seen it like this in years. the dry season is squeezing us
That sounds serious. How low are we talking — is it still flowing, or has it gone to standing pools? And is this the same stream your community depends on for household water, or mainly for the farm?

it is flowing but barely. you can step across it now. normally at this point in the year it is knee deep. the women use it for washing and the animals drink there too. the farm I do not irrigate but the nursery seedlings need water

If the stream is still flowing, you have time, but you need to be careful with how you use what's left. A few things to consider:

1. **Prioritize the nursery seedlings** — they are your future trees. Use small amounts of water directly at the root, not overhead. Morning watering only.
2. **Reduce any unnecessary water use** from the stream. If people are using it for laundry, they might consider doing that less frequently or using collected rainwater if any is stored.
3. **Mulch heavily** around the seedlings to reduce evaporation.

How far off is the rainy season typically for your area?

the major rains should come by April. maybe late March if Nyame is kind. that is 2 to 4 weeks. the seedlings I have been mulching already yes. cocoa husk and dried grass. but I have 200 seedlings in the nursery and they drink

200 seedlings is a significant investment to protect. Two to four weeks is manageable if you're strategic.

For 200 seedlings, try this approach:

- **Group watering**: if your seedlings are in polybags, push them closer together so you lose less water to the gaps between them. The shade they cast on each other also reduces evaporation.
- **Water every other day** instead of daily. Young cocoa seedlings can handle a brief dry period if the mulch is good. Better to give a deep soak every 48 hours than a light splash daily.
- **Shade structure**: if you don't have one, even palm fronds laid across sticks above the nursery will cut water loss significantly.

Are the seedlings under any shade currently?

yes I have a palm frond cover over them. but the fronds are old now and some have blown away. I will replace them tomorrow. the every other day idea is good. I have been watering daily and my back suffers for it

Your back will thank you. And the seedlings won't know the difference if the mulch is holding moisture.

When you replace the fronds, overlap them well so you get at least 60-70% shade coverage. Full sun on a nursery in dry season is your biggest enemy — it bakes the polybags and the roots cook.

How far is the water source from the nursery?
the stream is maybe 300 metres from the nursery. I carry with buckets. many trips. it is the walking that tires me not the watering itself

300 metres with buckets is exhausting when you repeat it many times. Could you place a large barrel or drum near the nursery? Fill it in the morning with a few bucket trips, then distribute water from the barrel with a smaller container. That way you walk the 300 metres three or four times instead of ten or twelve.
I have a drum. an old oil drum. I could roll it to the nursery and fill it. that is clever my friend. fill the drum in the morning then water from there. I do not know why I did not think of it

Sometimes the simple ideas hide in plain sight. The drum is perfect. Fill it once — maybe three or four bucket trips — and then you have water at the nursery for two days of watering without walking back and forth.

Make sure the drum is covered when you're not using it. Even a piece of plywood or a sheet of plastic over the top will stop evaporation from stealing your water.
I will cover it yes. and I should tell the other farmers in the village who have nurseries to do the same. some of them are struggling too. the dry season is hard on everyone not only me

That is the right instinct. If their seedlings die, the whole community loses future production. Share the drum idea and the every-other-day watering schedule. Simple advice that costs nothing but saves a lot.

You might also suggest that anyone with a nursery near the stream coordinates their watering times, so nobody is drawing too much at once. The stream needs to keep flowing.
yes. we should take turns. I will raise it at the next meeting. when the stream dries for one it dries for all. obi nnim when exactly the rain will come but we prepare and we wait. medaase my friend

That is a true saying. Worry without action is useless, but worry with a plan is wisdom — and you have a plan now. The drum, the mulch, the shade, the schedule, and a meeting to organize the village. Your seedlings are in good hands. The rains will come. Good evening.
the farmer who worries is the farmer who survives. good evening my friend
That he is. Take care and go easy on that back tomorrow. Good night.
